era_context: The Late Bronze Age in the Eastern Mediterranean and Near East. Thutmose III of Egypt was in the midst of his long and successful reign (c. 1479-1425 BCE). The Hittite Empire was consolidating power in Anatolia. Mycenaean civilization was flourishing in the Aegean. This was a period of relative stability among the great powers, with competition managed through military campaigns and diplomatic negotiation.
geographic_coverage_gaps: East Asia (Chinese records are sparse for this specific year)
The Americas (no written records; archaeological dating is imprecise)
Northern Europe and the Steppe (pre-literate or poorly documented)

events.title: Thutmose III's continued dominance in the Eastern Mediterranean
events.description: Thutmose III maintained Egypt's dominance in the Eastern Mediterranean during this period. His military campaigns had established Egyptian hegemony over the Levant, and his administrative reforms had strengthened Egypt's internal structure. The pharaoh was at the height of his power, and Egypt was experiencing a period of prosperity and cultural flourishing. The cult of Amun was becoming increasingly important, and the priesthood of Amun was accumulating wealth and power.

sources.name: Annals of Thutmose III, Temple of Amun at Karnak

events.certainty_note: Thutmose III's dominance is well-documented, but the precise year-by-year chronology is uncertain. This year is assigned based on conventional Egyptian chronology.
cross_references: Part of the broader Thutmosid period

events.title: Mycenaean palatial civilization at its height
events.description: The Mycenaean civilization of mainland Greece was at the height of its palatial period during this time. The major palace centers at Mycenae, Pylos, and Tiryns were thriving centers of political, economic, and cultural activity. These palaces controlled extensive territories and trade networks. The palatial economy was based on centralized redistribution of goods and labor organization. This period represents the apex of Mycenaean civilization before the disruptions of the Late Bronze Age Collapse.
key_figures: Mycenaean palace rulers (names unknown from this period)
events.certainty_note: The general prosperity of Mycenaean civilization during this period is well-established through archaeology. However, specific events or conditions of this particular year cannot be identified.
cross_references: Part of the Late Helladic II period (c. 1450-1400 BCE)

events.title: Hittite expansion and consolidation in Anatolia
events.description: The Hittite Empire was expanding its control over Anatolia and establishing itself as a major power in the Near East. The Hittites were developing the military and administrative structures that would characterize their empire. They were known for their military innovations and their use of written treaties to establish vassal relationships. The exact identity of the ruling Hittite king at this time is uncertain due to chronological debates.
events.certainty_note: The general pattern of Hittite expansion is well-established, but the precise chronology and specific events of this year are uncertain.
cross_references: Part of the broader period of Hittite imperial expansion

disconfirming_evidence: The chronology of this period is fundamentally uncertain, with different scholars proposing dates that differ by 50-100 years or more. The conventional chronology used here is based on the work of scholars like Kenneth Kitchen, but alternative chronologies would place these events at different dates. Additionally, the extent of Egyptian control over the Levant is debated—some scholars argue that Egyptian hegemony was less complete than temple inscriptions suggest.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 1433 BCE is heavily dependent on Egyptian temple inscriptions, which are biased toward emphasizing pharaonic success. Hittite records provide a counterpoint but are fragmentary and difficult to date. Archaeological evidence provides material context but cannot be tied to specific years without textual anchors. The absence of contemporary written records from most of the world means that the 'world history' of this year is heavily skewed toward the Eastern Mediterranean. Chronological uncertainty is the fundamental problem affecting all dating for this period.

graph_edges.note: Part of the same period of Thutmose III's dominance
Mycenaean civilization was flourishing throughout this period
